SEATTLE, WASH, July s.-The
Rockefeller and Hartman interests
are preparing to invade the territory
Of the Morgan and Hill combine and
give them a taste of competition in
the territory in which they have al
ways had a monopoly. Since the dis
solution of the Northern Securities
company the Hartman interests have
been preparing for an invasion of the
territory occupied by Mr.. Hill and,
II Hartman can not get control Of
the Northern Pacific railroad. he will
extend the Union and Southern Pacific
to Seattle.


On October 4. 1909. the stockhold-
ers of the Chicago, Milwaukee & st.
Paul railroad voted an increase in
the stock of the company by S25,0oo,-
oO0, but no reason was given for this
ed that the road was to be built to
the coast. Those watching the rail-
road horizon have been watching for
some move on the part of the Mil
waukee to indicate where II intends
to spend its $4,000. 900. but nothing
has appeared until agents of the road
began to buy tide lands in Seattle and
Tacoma.


The Chicago. Milwaukee & ST. Paul
road now reaches as far west as
Evarts and Chamberlain. s. ID. The
project as now outlined is to build
the road from Evarts westward to
meet the Tacoma Eastern, which has
been acquired by the Rockefeller In
terests. and thus give the oil and
railroad magnate open competition
with the Northern Pacific and Great
Northern, which are Morgan and Hill
roads.


Rockefeller owns a controlling in
terest In the roads operated under the
Hartman combination. He has sue
ceeded In getting a monopoly of the
western field south of the Columbia
river. but has left the northwest open
to the monopoly of the Morgan-Hill
railroads. His pet road is the Chi
cago, Milwaukee & ST. Paul and Il is
this road that he desires to build
to the coast. The Standard OH COM
pany has an immense acreage OF oil
lands in Alaska. Development on this
property has been delayed until some
plan can he carried out by which
Rockefeller can ship his oil over his
own road.


Oil Tanks on the Sound.


He must necessarily have great
tanks and refineries on the Puget
sound, because Portland does not Of
fer a safe harbor for large steamers.
such as would be employed In convey
ing the products OF his oil fields in
Alaska to a market in America. San
Francisco is too far south. and, con-
seduently the Puget sound is the place
where he must have his Pacific coast
headquarters for the Alaska oil in
dustry.
